narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 72.9% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your placement in the lowest decile for grades in your third year of school, which stood out as the strongest signal pushing the model toward forecasting failure—far below the higher deciles typical of students who passed the bar. Your first-year grades, also in the bottom decile, reinforced this concern, as they similarly deviated from the stronger performances seen in successful bar takers. While your LSAT score of 35 worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward success, it wasn't enough to offset those early academic hurdles, especially since it fell short of the average around 37 that passers usually have. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the mark for admitted students at 3.24, but it didn't carry enough weight to change the overall outlook. Coming from an upper-middle income family and planning full-time studies aligns well with patterns among bar passers, yet the model prioritized your decile rankings as the key risks. Compared to typical accepted students, who often rank in the middle or top deciles early on, your position at the bottom in both first and third years created a stark contrast that dominated the decision. These lower rankings suggest the model saw potential challenges in sustaining the academic momentum needed for the bar. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with a solid LSAT and GPA in hand, but this highlights where your profile diverged most from proven paths to success. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional undergrad-level work to demonstrate growth. Exploring part-time options or building a stronger narrative around your upper-middle background could also help in reapplying. Many students in similar spots have improved their profiles and succeeded—focusing on those academic deciles could be your best next step.
